checks.a_analysis: The HTML rendering follows the visual organization of the source PDF. Each slide from the PDF is represented as a distinct section in the HTML. The layout transitions from the presentation format of the PDF to a structured web document while maintaining the logical flow of information. The relationship between headers, text blocks, and images remains consistent with the source's intent.

The data tables, such as the 'Improvement from Stop Consolidation' table on PDF page 16 and the 'School Boundary Planning' tally on PDF page 17, are correctly rendered as HTML tables. They maintain their columnar structure, headers are distinct, and the data remains readable and aligned. The information presented in the 'Student Data from PowerSchool' screenshots (PDF page 9) is also successfully converted into a structured table format in the HTML.

All meaningful images from the source PDF are present in the HTML rendering. This includes the logo in the header, the school bus photos (pages 1, 2, 3, 11), the historical digitizing photos (page 5), the pen plotter photos (page 6), the GIS software screenshots (pages 7, 8, 9, 13, 17), the maps (pages 12, 14, 15, 22, 23), and the charts (page 19). No broken image icons or missing content elements were observed.

There is no evidence of body content duplication. Each slide's content appears exactly once in the body of the HTML document. While titles appear in the navigation/TOC area and again in the body, this is a standard accessibility feature and not a defect.
